questions.question: According to Semendeferi et al. (1997, 2002), what is the proportional size of the prefrontal cortex in humans relative to great apes?

options.A: Humans have a disproportionately larger PFC (~50%) than great apes
options.B: Roughly equal at ~35% in both humans and great apes
options.C: Humans have a smaller PFC than great apes when corrected for body size
options.D: Humans have ~70% PFC vs ~20% in great apes

questions.explanation: Semendeferi et al. revised Brodmann's older estimates using structural MRI and found the proportional frontal lobe size is roughly equal (~35%) in humans and great apes. Humans are not distinctive because of an oversized PFC but because of overall brain scaling.

questions.question: What is the canonical metaphor Miller & Cohen (2001) used to describe the role of the PFC?

options.A: A central processor that stores all rules
options.B: A railroad switch operator that strengthens some pathways and inhibits others
options.C: A library that retrieves stored memories on demand
options.D: A passive filter that blocks irrelevant input

questions.explanation: Miller & Cohen (2001) likened PFC to a railroad switch operator — it sets up patterns of information flow, strengthening context-appropriate pathways and inhibiting others. Their classic example: a phone rings, you answer at home but ignore at the neighbour's.

questions.question: In Wallis, Anderson & Miller (2001), what was the key finding about rule-selective neurons in lateral PFC?

options.A: Each neuron fired only for a specific stimulus, regardless of rule
options.B: Neurons fired selectively for a particular rule, independent of cue and stimulus
options.C: Neurons only fired during the motor response phase
options.D: Rule coding was found exclusively in the OFC, not lateral PFC

questions.explanation: Wallis, Anderson & Miller (2001) recorded single units in lateral PFC and found neurons fired selectively for a particular rule (matching vs non-matching), independent of the cue (juice or tone) and the stimulus (Object A or B). Rule-selective neurons cluster along the principal sulcus.

questions.question: Brenda Milner's (1963) classic finding about Wisconsin Card Sorting Test (WCST) performance in PFC patients was that perseveration is:

options.A: A learning deficit — patients cannot learn new rules
options.B: A failure to disengage from a previously valid rule, not a learning deficit
options.C: Caused by basal ganglia damage, not PFC damage
options.D: Only present when feedback is delayed

questions.explanation: Milner (1963) reported PFC patients perseverate on the WCST, but the prof emphasised this is NOT a learning deficit. Patients learn new rules just fine when the old rule is unavailable; the deficit is specifically inability to disengage from the old, still-applicable rule.

questions.question: Damage to which region most reliably impairs the stop-signal task and is implicated in ADHD impulse-control deficits?
questions.explanation: Right ventrolateral PFC (rVL-PFC) damage selectively impairs cancelling an already-initiated response on the stop-signal task. The same pattern appears in ADHD and Parkinson's, linking rVL-PFC to impulse control.

questions.question: Which finding most directly challenged the original conflict-monitoring model of the ACC?

options.A: Kerns et al. (2004) showed ACC activation predicts dlPFC adjustment
options.B: Fellows & Farah (2005) found dorsal-ACC lesion patients have a normal Stroop effect
options.C: Posner & Petersen (1990) identified three control systems
options.D: Bush, Luu & Posner (2000) distinguished dorsal from rostral ACC

questions.explanation: Fellows & Farah (2005) showed that patients with dorsal ACC lesions perform normally on the Stroop and go/no-go tasks (Stroop effect identical to controls). If ACC were the conflict detector, lesions should abolish the effect — this directly challenged the original model.

questions.question: In the Funahashi, Bruce & Goldman-Rakic (1989) oculomotor delayed response task, what did they discover about dlPFC neurons?

options.A: Neurons fired only at stimulus onset and offset
options.B: Neurons showed sustained, content-specific delay-period activity with memory fields
options.C: Neurons inhibited each other to suppress motor responses
options.D: Neurons coded reward magnitude rather than spatial location

questions.explanation: Funahashi, Bruce & Goldman-Rakic (1989) showed dlPFC neurons fire continuously through the 3-second delay (delay-period activity), content-specific via memory fields (preferred location). When firing broke down, monkeys made errors — the canonical neural mechanism for working memory.

questions.question: According to Petrides, what is the functional dissociation within lateral PFC for working memory?

options.C: Both regions handle maintenance equally; OFC handles manipulation
options.D: dlPFC handles both, vlPFC is irrelevant for working memory

questions.explanation: Petrides proposed vlPFC supports maintenance (simple 'hold this letter'), while dlPFC supports manipulation (alphabetise, reorder, n-back). dlPFC activation scales with cognitive demand (D'Esposito et al. 2000), while vlPFC stays roughly constant.

questions.question: Harrison & Tong (2009) used multivoxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to demonstrate that working memory content is stored in:

options.B: The hippocampus during the delay period
options.C: Early visual cortex (V1-V4), the same regions that processed the stimulus
options.D: The basal ganglia via the prefrontal loop

questions.explanation: Harrison & Tong (2009) decoded the orientation of a remembered grating from V1-V4 activity using MVPA, supporting the distributed network view: WM content lives in the same sensory regions that processed it. PFC orchestrates via top-down signals but does not store the content.

questions.question: Cools, Clark & Robbins (2004) found that during task switching, the basal ganglia activate for:

options.A: All types of switches, including abstract rule switches
options.B: Only abstract rule switches, not object switches
options.C: Only object switches, while dlPFC activates for all switches
options.D: Neither type of switch — only the OFC was involved

questions.explanation: Cools, Clark & Robbins (2004) showed basal ganglia activate only for object switches (stimulus-response level), while dlPFC activates for all switches. The BG handles concrete S-R rules; PFC handles abstract rules — fitting the basal ganglia loops architecture.
